content.text: Security OF the Cloud — AWS's Responsibilities
AWS operates under a Shared Responsibility Model that divides security obligations between AWS and its customers. AWS is responsible for "
" — the foundational layers that customers cannot access directly: physical hardware, the hypervisor, and the global network. Customers are responsible for "
" — everything they deploy on top of that foundation. Understanding exactly what AWS owns is critical for knowing what controls you can assume are in place and which you must implement yourself.

attrs.alt: Shared Responsibility Model: AWS security OF the cloud responsibilities and inherited compliance certifications

content.text: AWS designs, builds, and operates its own data centers — it does not lease commodity colocation space. This vertical integration allows AWS to control the entire physical security stack from the building perimeter to the server rack.

Data centers have no exterior signage identifying them as AWS facilities. Physical access requires multi-factor authentication (badge + biometric), and access is granted only to employees with a specific business need. Video surveillance, intrusion detection, and 24/7 security guards monitor every facility.

AWS maintains a documented chain of custody for all server hardware from procurement to decommissioning. Custom hardware designs (including the Nitro card and Graviton processors) reduce reliance on third-party firmware stacks that could introduce supply-chain vulnerabilities.

When a storage device reaches end-of-life, AWS uses NIST 800-88 (Guidelines for Media Sanitization) compliant procedures — multiple-pass overwrite or physical destruction — before the device leaves the facility. Customers cannot retrieve data from recycled hardware.

Data centers are designed for N+1 redundancy on all critical systems: uninterruptible power supplies (UPS), backup generators with on-site fuel, and multiple independent cooling systems. These are physical plant concerns that AWS handles transparently.

Nitro is AWS's custom hardware and software platform that underlies EC2. Understanding Nitro is important because it explains the security guarantees AWS can make about hypervisor isolation — guarantees that are stronger than traditional software-based virtualization.

A dedicated hardware card in each server that handles all I/O operations (networking, storage) in hardware rather than software. The hypervisor no longer needs to participate in the I/O data path, dramatically reducing the attack surface of the virtualization layer.

A minimal hypervisor purpose-built for security and performance. It provides CPU and memory isolation between instances. Because the hypervisor does not handle I/O, it is significantly simpler (fewer lines of code) than traditional hypervisors like Xen, making it easier to verify and audit.

An AWS-designed chip on the motherboard that provides a cryptographic hardware root of trust. It verifies the integrity of the BIOS and bootloader on every boot. If firmware is tampered with between power cycles, the chip detects the change.

Memory belonging to one customer's instance is never readable by another, even by the hypervisor. CPU side-channel mitigations (for Spectre/Meltdown variants) are implemented at the hardware level on Nitro-based instance types.

An optional feature that creates an isolated compute environment inside an EC2 instance with no persistent storage, no external networking, and no SSH access. Designed for processing highly sensitive data (private keys, PII, medical records) where even the instance owner should not be able to access the enclave memory.

AWS operates one of the world's largest private global networks — a dedicated fiber backbone connecting all AWS regions, Availability Zones, and edge locations. Traffic between AWS facilities does not traverse the public internet.

AWS has deployed hundreds of thousands of miles of fiber and maintains private peering relationships at every major internet exchange point. This gives AWS-to-AWS traffic predictable, low-latency, and high-bandwidth paths.

AWS operates 30+ regions globally, each consisting of 3 or more Availability Zones. AZs are physically separate data centers 1–100 km apart, connected by high-bandwidth, low-latency dedicated links. They share no single points of failure for power or cooling.

600+ CloudFront Points of Presence (PoPs) and Regional Edge Caches bring content and processing closer to end users. AWS Outposts extends AWS infrastructure to customer-managed facilities for use cases requiring data residency or extremely low latency to on-premises systems.

All AWS customers receive Shield Standard at no charge. It protects against common network-layer (L3) and transport-layer (L4) DDoS attacks — SYN/ACK floods, UDP reflection, ICMP floods — automatically and without customer configuration. AWS absorbs attack traffic at scale before it reaches customer resources.

AWS continuously monitors its network infrastructure with automated anomaly detection. Volumetric attacks are identified and mitigated within seconds by AWS's global scrubbing infrastructure. Customers never see the mitigation in action — it is transparent.

The scope of AWS's responsibility expands significantly for managed services. When you use a managed service, AWS takes on operating system patching, database engine upgrades, and runtime maintenance — responsibilities that fall on you when you self-manage on EC2.

This shift of operational responsibility is one of the primary value propositions of managed services. A team managing self-hosted PostgreSQL on EC2 must track CVEs, apply patches in maintenance windows, and test upgrades. With RDS, AWS handles those tasks and notifies you only when a maintenance window will cause a brief interruption — which you can reschedule.

AWS maintains a large portfolio of third-party compliance certifications and audit reports. When you build on AWS, you inherit the portions of these certifications that apply to the infrastructure layer — you do not need to re-audit the data center or hypervisor. You only need to demonstrate that
configurations and controls meet the standard's requirements for the customer-responsibility side.

System and Organization Controls reports. SOC 2 Type II covers security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy over a 12-month period. AWS makes SOC 3 publicly available; SOC 2 requires signing an NDA and requesting through AWS Artifact.

International standards for information security management systems. ISO 27017 covers cloud-specific controls; ISO 27018 covers PII in public clouds. Audited annually by a third-party certification body.

Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard. AWS infrastructure is certified as a PCI Level 1 Service Provider, the highest level. Services listed in scope for PCI-DSS can be used to build cardholder data environments — but your application must also be PCI-compliant.

Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act. AWS signs a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with healthcare customers. HIPAA eligibility is a subset of AWS services; see the AWS HIPAA-eligible services list before deploying PHI.

Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program. AWS GovCloud (US) regions hold FedRAMP High authorization for US government workloads. Commercial regions have FedRAMP Moderate authorizations for many services.
A self-service portal in the AWS Console where you download audit reports, certifications, and agreements (including BAAs and NDAs) on demand. No need to contact AWS to get the latest SOC 2 report.
Understanding the limits of AWS's responsibility is equally important. AWS secures the infrastructure but makes no guarantees about:

A vulnerable web application running on AWS will be exploited just as readily as one running on-premises. AWS does not scan your application code, inspect your API payloads, or block application-layer attacks unless you deploy WAF.
Overly permissive IAM policies are one of the most common causes of AWS security incidents. AWS provides the IAM tools but is not responsible for how you configure them.

AWS provides encryption capabilities for every storage service, but encryption at rest is not enabled by default on all services in all configurations. You must configure it — and choose the right key management approach.

Security groups default to denying all inbound traffic, which is secure by default. But if you open a security group to 0.0.0.0/0 on port 22, that is your misconfiguration, not an AWS infrastructure failure.

On EC2, the guest operating system is entirely the customer's responsibility. An unpatched Log4Shell or OpenSSL vulnerability on an EC2 instance is the customer's problem — AWS will not apply guest OS patches.
If you can see it in the AWS Console or touch it with an API, you are responsible for securing it. If it runs under the hood — the hypervisor, the physical server, the fiber — that is AWS's domain.

One of the most practical benefits of building on AWS is the inheritance of physical and infrastructure controls for compliance purposes. Before cloud adoption, organizations seeking PCI-DSS certification had to audit their own data center — physical access controls, server hardware provenance, network cabling, and disk decommissioning procedures. On AWS, those controls are pre-audited and documented.

An auditor examining a PCI-compliant application on AWS can rely on AWS's PCI DSS Attestation of Compliance (AoC) for infrastructure-layer controls. The audit scope shrinks to application-layer and customer-configuration controls.
AWS's certifications are renewed on a regular cadence (SOC 2 annually, ISO yearly). As long as you use in-scope services, your inherited controls remain current without customer action.

AWS Compliance Center and AWS Artifact provide whitepapers, FAQs, and mapping documents that show which AWS services satisfy which controls in NIST 800-53, ISO 27001, SOC 2, and dozens of other frameworks.
AWS Security Operations and Threat Intelligence

AWS operates a 24/7 global Security Operations Center (SOC) with hundreds of security engineers dedicated solely to monitoring and defending the AWS infrastructure. Customers benefit from this investment without staffing or funding it themselves.

Because AWS sees network traffic and activity patterns across millions of customers and tens of billions of API calls per day, its threat intelligence is fundamentally different from what any single organization could collect. AWS uses this data to identify new attack patterns and update mitigations automatically — improvements that immediately benefit all customers.

AWS has its own internal security team (Amazon Security) that researches vulnerabilities, coordinates responsible disclosure, and prioritizes patches for the services AWS manages. AWS Security Bulletins publish information about critical vulnerabilities and the timeline for patching managed services.

AWS runs a vulnerability disclosure program through HackerOne for its customer-facing services. External researchers who discover and responsibly disclose vulnerabilities in AWS services help harden the infrastructure for all customers.

Customers may perform penetration testing against their own AWS resources (EC2, RDS, CloudFront, API Gateway, Lambda, Lightsail) without prior approval for most services. Certain disruptive activities (port flooding, DDoS simulation, DNS zone walking) require pre-approval through an AWS vulnerability and penetration testing request form.
Availability Zone Design and Fault Isolation

AWS Availability Zones are the primary unit of high-availability design. Understanding how AWS engineers AZs illuminates both the infrastructure guarantees you receive and the architectural decisions you must make to leverage them.

Each AZ consists of one or more physically separate data centers with independent power feeds from separate substations, independent cooling systems, and independent network uplinks. A power outage, flooding, or fire in one AZ is designed not to propagate to adjacent AZs.

AZs within a region are connected by dedicated, fully redundant, low-latency (single-digit millisecond) fiber links operated entirely within AWS's network. This enables synchronous database replication and stateful failover between AZs.

AWS conducts "blast radius" analysis when designing features, specifically engineering to limit the scope of failures. Not every service failure can be contained to a single AZ, but AZ-scoped failures are the default failure model — multi-region failures are rare and historically brief.

For workloads needing latency below what a region can provide (autonomous vehicles, AR/VR, industrial control systems), AWS operates Local Zones in metropolitan areas and Wavelength zones embedded in carrier networks — all still under AWS's physical infrastructure management.
AWS's network infrastructure incorporates security controls that protect all tenants simultaneously, independent of any configuration customers apply.

AWS applies anti-spoofing filters at the network boundary — an EC2 instance cannot send packets with a source IP other than its own assigned IP. This eliminates a class of amplification attacks (e.g., DNS reflection) that require spoofing the victim's IP as the source address.
Inter-tenant isolation at the hardware level:

Nitro hardware enforces network isolation between instances. An instance cannot send traffic directly to another instance's network interface — all routing must pass through AWS's virtualization layer, which enforces security group rules.

Every EC2 instance has per-instance bandwidth limits enforced in hardware (Nitro). An instance cannot consume more network bandwidth than its type allows, preventing a single tenant from monopolizing shared physical network capacity.
Encryption of data in transit between AZs:

Traffic traversing AWS's backbone network between Availability Zones is automatically encrypted at the physical layer. This means data moving between your RDS primary in us-east-1a and its standby replica in us-east-1b is encrypted in transit without any customer action.

# Verify which network-level security controls AWS enforces vs what you configure
# AWS enforces at Nitro HW level (cannot be changed by customer):
#   - Anti-spoofing (src IP validation)
#   - Instance bandwidth limits
#   - Inter-instance traffic isolation
#   - AZ backbone encryption

# Customer configures in software (security groups, NACLs):
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-ids sg-0abc123 \
  --query "SecurityGroups[0].IpPermissions"
Validating AWS's Security Posture: Third-Party Audits

AWS's security claims are validated by independent third-party auditors on a continuous or annual basis. Unlike marketing claims, these certifications require documented controls, evidence collection, and independent testing.

Respected third-party audit firms including Ernst & Young CertifyPoint, A-lign, Coalfire, and the BSI Group conduct AWS's ISO, SOC, and PCI-DSS audits. The same standards bodies and auditors that certify financial institutions and healthcare providers assess AWS.

Not every AWS service is in scope for every certification. The AWS Services in Scope by Compliance Program page is the authoritative list. Before deploying regulated workloads, verify that the specific services you use are in scope for your required certification.

Under several programs (CAIQ — Cloud Security Alliance Consensus Assessments Initiative Questionnaire), AWS publishes completed questionnaires that your information security team can use instead of sending AWS a vendor assessment.

You cannot independently audit AWS data centers (no customer site visits). Instead, you rely on the published third-party audit reports. This is the standard model for major cloud providers and is acceptable under most regulatory frameworks.

# Download AWS compliance artifacts programmatically via AWS Artifact
# (requires accepting the NDA for SOC 2 / PCI reports)
aws artifact get-report-metadata \
  --report-id "SOC2TypeII_2024" \
  --term-token $ACCEPTED_TERM_TOKEN

# Or list available agreements for your account
aws artifact list-agreements \
  --query "agreements[?agreementType=='BAA'].{Name:displayName, Status:status}"
How AWS Responsibilities Change Across Service Models

The division of responsibility shifts based on the service type. The higher-level the service, the more AWS manages — but also the less visibility and control you have into the underlying implementation.
AWS Infrastructure as a Security Control
AWS's infrastructure itself provides security controls that are difficult or impossible to replicate on-premises at the same cost. Three examples stand out:

On EC2, the Nitro hypervisor provides cryptographically enforced memory isolation between instances. Even the AWS hypervisor cannot read your instance's RAM. On traditional shared hardware (bare-metal colocation), memory isolation is a software guarantee from the hypervisor vendor — not a hardware guarantee. This matters for workloads processing sensitive data like encryption keys or PII.

AWS's network capacity (measured in terabits per second) exceeds the capacity of any known DDoS attack. Shield Standard absorbs volumetric attacks that would overwhelm an on-premises data center's upstream bandwidth — attacks that historically required specialized scrubbing center subscriptions costing tens of thousands of dollars per month.
Global geographic distribution without global operations:

AWS lets a three-person startup deploy in 30 regions with the same physical security posture as a Fortune 500's data center — without managing any of the physical infrastructure. The operational leverage here is enormous: AWS's economy of scale funds the same security engineering that major financial institutions employ.

Environmental threats — fire, flood, power outage, overheating — are as dangerous to data availability as cyberattacks. AWS data center design addresses each with multiple redundant systems, and AWS is fully accountable for these controls.

AWS data centers use early warning smoke detection and non-water-based fire suppression systems (typically FM-200 or Novec 1230 clean agents) that suppress fire without damaging servers or creating water damage. Systems are tested regularly.

Data center construction accounts for regional geological and meteorological risks. AWS does not publish specific design parameters but data center standards (Uptime Institute Tier III/IV) govern the resilience requirements. Coastal regions use flood barriers; seismic zones use vibration-damped racks.

A typical AWS data center operates at critical power loads well above 10 megawatts. UPS systems provide seconds to minutes of ride-through during utility power interruption; on-site diesel generators provide hours to days of backup. Fuel contracts ensure generator replenishment during extended outages.

Every rack in an AWS data center is instrumented with temperature sensors. Automated cooling controls adjust airflow and chilling capacity in real time. Overheating conditions that could damage servers are prevented before they occur, not remediated after.

Modern hardware supply chains involve dozens of vendors across multiple countries. A compromised server component — a malicious BIOS, a backdoored network card, a tampered PCIe device — could undermine all software-layer security controls. AWS's approach to supply chain security is unusually comprehensive for an enterprise customer and is itself a security control customers inherit.

AWS designs its own chips (Graviton processors, Nitro cards, Trainium/Inferentia accelerators) specifically to reduce the attack surface and supply chain risk associated with third-party silicon. A chip AWS designed and had fabricated at a trusted foundry has a smaller attack surface than a commodity CPU with a large firmware stack.

The Nitro Security Chip cryptographically verifies firmware integrity on every boot, detecting unauthorized modifications to BIOS, UEFI, or BMC firmware — a class of attack (firmware implant) that is extraordinarily difficult to detect at the OS or application level.

AWS maintains strict controls over hardware component sourcing and logistics. Components are tracked from manufacturer to data center installation. This doesn't eliminate all supply chain risk, but it is orders of magnitude more rigorous than what most organizations can afford.

Where hardware TPMs are used, AWS employs them to extend the chain of trust from the Nitro Security Chip through the full boot sequence. This enables attestation — proving to an external verifier that a specific, unmodified software stack is running on specific, verified hardware.
AWS Compliance and Regulatory Programs by Industry

Different industries face different regulatory frameworks. AWS maintains certifications and compliance programs across a broad set of regulatory regimes, allowing customers to use AWS for even highly regulated workloads.
Use Financial Services Lens of Well-Architected
Must use only HIPAA-eligible services for PHI
GovCloud is physically separated from commercial
Data stays in EU regions when configured
For most regulated industries, using AWS does not by itself satisfy regulatory requirements — you must

configure your AWS environment correctly and document your controls. AWS's role is to provide the infrastructure foundation and evidence you can reference. The AWS Compliance Center (aws.amazon.com/compliance) provides jurisdiction-specific and industry-specific whitepapers explaining exactly how AWS's shared responsibility model maps to each framework's requirements.
Practical Implications for Teams New to AWS
Understanding what AWS handles automatically helps new AWS teams focus their security effort on the controls that actually require their attention.

Physical server procurement and decommissioning. Rack-and-stack procedures. BIOS patching on managed services. Data center access audits. Power and cooling redundancy engineering. These are zero-effort items on AWS — the infrastructure team simply never deals with them.

IAM role design and privilege minimization. VPC network segmentation. Security group rule reviews. S3 bucket policy audits. CloudTrail and GuardDuty configuration. Application security testing and patch management for anything running on EC2.

When preparing for a compliance audit, lead with the AWS certifications you inherit. Then scope your audit to demonstrate only the controls in the customer-responsibility zone. Auditors familiar with AWS will already know the infrastructure controls are covered — help them navigate directly to where your configurations live.
Use AWS Config rules for continuous evidence:

Compliance evidence is not just a point-in-time snapshot — it requires continuous monitoring. AWS Config rules that flag non-compliant resources (e.g., S3 bucket without default encryption, security group allowing 0.0.0.0/0 on port 22) generate an ongoing record that demonstrates continuous compliance monitoring for auditors.

A common concern when adopting cloud is the opacity of the underlying infrastructure — customers cannot physically inspect AWS data centers. AWS addresses this through a layered transparency model that provides evidence without granting physical access.

AWS publishes information about infrastructure operations including law enforcement data requests (Government Information Request Reports), infrastructure commitment to security and privacy, and details about the Nitro System's security architecture.

AWS regularly publishes detailed technical whitepapers on how specific security controls work — including the Nitro system security model, KMS hardware security, and CloudHSM design. These whitepapers are reviewed and updated as architecture evolves.

AWS participates in threat intelligence sharing communities and coordinates vulnerability disclosures with CERT/CC and national cybersecurity agencies (CISA in the US, BSI in Germany, NCSC in the UK). Infrastructure-level vulnerabilities (like Spectre/Meltdown) were coordinated with AWS before public disclosure so patches could be pre-deployed.
Third-party validation as a substitute for access:

The model of 'trust but verify through auditors' is not unique to cloud — it is how publicly traded companies handle financial audits, how nuclear plants handle safety inspections, and how pharmaceutical manufacturers handle FDA inspections. An unannounced audit by a qualified third party provides higher assurance than a customer site visit.
AWS owns the entire physical layer:
Data center construction and security, server hardware design (including Nitro), disk decommissioning, power/cooling redundancy — customers have no access to or responsibility for any of this.

AWS's custom hypervisor and hardware root-of-trust give EC2 instance isolation stronger guarantees than traditional software hypervisors. Memory and CPU side-channels are mitigated at the hardware level.
Managed services shift more responsibility to AWS:

Using RDS, Lambda, or DynamoDB instead of self-managing on EC2 means AWS patches the OS and runtime. The farther up the stack you go (IaaS → PaaS → SaaS), the less infrastructure security work remains for you.

Building on AWS means your compliance audit can rely on AWS's SOC 2, PCI Level 1, ISO 27001, and other certifications for the infrastructure layer. You only need to demonstrate controls for the customer-responsibility zone.
AWS does not protect against your own misconfigurations:
No amount of AWS infrastructure security compensates for open security groups, public S3 buckets, overly permissive IAM roles, or unpatched application code. Those are yours to own.
